does: This paper addresses catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning by proposing DG-Hard, a post-hoc repair method that operates on weight differences alone. Given a base pretrained model and its fine-tuned variant, the method decomposes the fine-tuning update as a low-rank task signal embedded in high-dimensional noise. It applies Donoho-Gavish hard singular-value thresholding to weight-delta matrices, filtering away spectral bulk while preserving structured updates. The approach requires only the two checkpoints and no training data, relying on closed-form SVD operations to recover degraded capabilities while maintaining target-task performance and beneficial held-out improvements. The method treats the fine-tuning trajectory as a signal-recovery problem solvable through spectral filtering without data-dependent hyperparameter tuning.
connects: This work extends research on catastrophic forgetting in neural networks, a longstanding problem in continual learning where task-specific adaptation damages previously learned knowledge. It builds on classical ideas from signal processing and low-rank recovery, importing the Donoho-Gavish threshold from compressive sensing into the fine-tuning setting. The paper responds to limitations of prior repair approaches that either require expensive retraining, access to original data, or per-task tuning. It connects to recent observations that fine-tuning updates are often low-rank and that weight-space geometry encodes task information separately from general knowledge. The spectral perspective on neural network weights also relates to work analyzing redundancy and structure in overparameterized models.
enables: This approach enables practical workflows where damaged capabilities can be recovered without computational overhead or data access, making post-hoc repair viable for deployed systems. It opens directions for understanding which dimensions of the weight space correspond to task-specific versus general knowledge through spectral analysis. The method could facilitate multi-task adaptation by selectively recovering components lost during specialized fine-tuning, supporting modular model composition. Future work might extend spectral filtering to more complex update scenarios, combine it with other repair signals beyond checkpoints, or apply similar decomposition principles to understand interference between competing objectives. It also suggests avenues for interpreting fine-tuning dynamics through the lens of signal and noise in weight-delta matrices.
